concepts.title: Number System
concepts.conceptBody:
Elementary Number System 

The whole of calculus is based on the concepts of real numbers. So let us briefly discuss real numbers.

Terms & their definitions 

  • The numbers $-4,-3,-2,-1,0,1,2,3,4$ are called integers.

i.e. set of positive integers + zero + negative integers.

They are denoted by I or Z

  • Natural numbers : They are a subset of integers & denoted by $N$

$\mathrm{N}=\{1,2,3, \ldots$

i.e. all positive integers.

  • Whole numbers : It is also a subset of integers. It contains positive integers + zero.

Denoted by W

$\mathrm{W}=\{0,1,2,3 \ldots\}$

  • Zero : Zero is an integer but neither a positive nor a negative integer. But it

is non-negative as well as non-positive integer.

concepts.title: Types of intervals
concepts.conceptBody:
Intervals 

1. Open Interval : $\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }$ For two real numbers a and $b$, where $a<b$, the set of all real 

numbers lying strictly between a and b (i.e. not including a and b) is

called an open interval.

denoted by ( ) [round brackets]

i.e. $a<x<b\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,x\in \left( a,b \right)$

2. Closed interval : $\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }$ Again for same 2 real numbers, if $x$ can take values between a and

$\text{b}$ including a & b, then its a closed interval.

i.e. $\text{a}\le \text{x}\le \text{b }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\text{ }\,\,\,\text{x}\in \left[ \text{a},\text{b} \right]$ {square brackets are used}

3. Half Open Interval : It contains both type of intervals, open, closed interval & closed

open interval. In this type only one end point is included. 
$\text{a}<\text{x}\le \text{b}$ $\text{x}\in \left( \text{a},\text{b} \right]$ 
$\text{a}\le \text{x}<\text{b}$ $\text{x}\in \left[ \text{a},\text{b} \right)$

4. Infinite intervals : Before going to intervals let us discuss first about infinity, denoted

by $\infty$.

By infinity we mean that it is a very big real number, larger than

any real number but how large, it is not fixed.

When we say $x\in R$, we indirectly mean

$-\infty <\text{x}<\infty \text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ or }\!\!~\!\!\text{ x}\in \left( -\infty ,\infty \right)$

coming to infinite intervals now,

whenever $\pm \infty$ is at one or both the end points we never include

them; i.e.

$-\infty <\text{x}<\infty \text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ or }\!\!~\!\!\text{ x}\in \left( -\infty ,\infty \right)$

round brackets

Not square brackets 

$\begin{array}{*{35}{r}} -\infty <\text{x}<\text{a} & \text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ or }\!\!~\!\!\text{ } & \text{x}\in \left( -\infty ,\text{a} \right) \\ -\infty <\text{x}\le \text{a} & \text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ or }\!\!~\!\!\text{ } & \text{x}\in \left( -\infty ,\text{a} \right] \\ \text{x}\le \text{a} & \text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ or }\!\!~\!\!\text{ } & \text{x}\in \left( -\infty ,\text{a} \right] \\ \end{array}$

concepts.title: Function(mapping) with the help of sets
concepts.conceptBody:
Mapping

Definition : Let $\text{X}$ and $\text{Y}$ be two non-empty sets. A subset $\text{f}$ of $\text{X }\!\!~\!\!\text{ Y}$ is called a function

from $\text{X}$ to $\text{Y}$ iff to each $\text{x}\in \text{X}$, there exists a unique $\text{y}$ in $\text{Y}$ such that $\left( \text{x},\text{y} \right)\in \text{f}$.

The other terms used for functions are "mappings", transformations" and "operators". We

denote this mapping by

$\text{f}:\text{X}\to \text{Y}$ or $\text{X} \text{f}\,\text{Y}$

It follows from the above definition that a relation $\text{X}$ to $\text{Y}$ is a function from $\text{X}$ to $\text{Y}$ iff

(i) to each $x\in X$ there exists a $y\in Y$ such that $\left( x,y \right)\in f$,

(ii) $\left( \text{x},{{\text{y}}_{1}} \right)\in \text{f}$ and $\left( \text{x},{{\text{y}}_{2}} \right)\in \text{f}\Rightarrow {{\text{y}}_{1}}={{\text{y}}_{2}}$.

The condition (i) ensures that to each $\text{x}$ in $\text{X},\text{f}$ associates an element $\text{y}$ in $\text{Y}$ and

condition (ii) guarantees that $\text{y}$ is unique.

We call $\text{X}$, the domain of $\text{f}$ and $\text{Y}$ the co-domain of $\text{f}$. The unique element $\text{y}$ in $\text{Y}$ assigned to $\text{x}\in $ $X$ is called the image of $x$ under $f$ or the value of $f$ at $x$ and is denoted by $f\left( x \right)$. Also $x$ is called a pre-image (or inverse image) of $y$. Note that there may be more than one pre-images of $y$. The graph of $f$ is the subset of $X\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }Y$ defined by $\left\{ \left( x,f\left( x \right) \right):x\in X \right\}$ The range of $f$ is the set of all images under $\text{f}$ and is denoted by $\text{f}\left[ \text{X} \right]$. Thus

$\begin{array}{*{35}{r}} f\left[ X \right] & ~=\left\{ y\in Y:y=f\left( x \right)\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ for }\!\!~\!\!\text{ some }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }x\in X \right\} \\ {} & ~=\left\{ f\left( x \right):x\in X \right\}. \\ \end{array}$

If $\text{A}\subset \text{X}$, then the set $\left\{ \text{f}\left( \text{x} \right):\text{x}\in \text{A} \right\}$ is called the image of $\text{A}$ under $\text{f}$ and is

denoted by $\text{f}\left[ \text{A} \right]$. If $\text{B}\subset \text{Y}$; then the set $\left\{ \text{x}\in \text{X}:\text{f}\left( \text{x} \right)\in \text{B} \right\}$ is called the inverse

image of $\text{B}$ under $\text{f}$ and is denoted by ${{\text{f}}^{-1}}\left[ \text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ B} \right]$.

concepts.title: Relation is mapping or not
concepts.conceptBody:
How to Decide That a Relation is a mapping? 

(1) Draw graph of $y=f\left( x \right)$

(2) Draw vertical lines in domain

(3) Make pt(s) of intersection b/w vertical lines and graph.

(4) If every vertical line has exactly one pt. of intersection then $\text{f}\left( \text{x} \right)$ is a mapping.

e.g. $y=\text{log}x\left( R\to R \right)$

not a mapping.

concepts.title: Finding the curve of f(x)=maximum{g(x),h(x)....}
concepts.conceptBody:
To find the curve of $\text{f}\left( \text{x} \right)=\text{max}.\{\text{g}\left( \text{x} \right),\text{h}\left( \text{x} \right)\ldots .$. 

In this type we draw the curves of all functions like $\text{g}\left( \text{x} \right),\text{h}\left( \text{x} \right)\ldots $ and then we choose the

part of the curve which is at top (has max value of y) with respect to all other curves in that region.

Q) Find the function/curve of $f\left( x \right)=\text{max}.\left\{ {{x}^{2}},{{(1-x)}^{2}},2x\left( 1-x \right) \right\}$ 

Solution : 

First we will draw the graphs of each of the function ${{x}^{2}},{{(1-x)}^{2}},2x\left( 1-x \right)$ for $\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }y={{x}^{2}}$

 

Now we will combine all the curves in one curve

Now mark the points which are above other curves

We just now have to find the intersection points (A) & (B) for (A) its between

Curves $y=2x\left( 1-x \right)\And y={{x}^{2}}$

Equating $2x\left( 1-x \right)={{x}^{2}}$

as we know the other point, i.e. $x=0$

$\begin{array}{*{35}{r}} {} & 2-2\text{x}=\text{x} \\ {} & ~\Rightarrow \text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ x}=2/3 \\ {} & \text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ for }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }B\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ its }\!\!~\!\!\text{ between }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }y={{(1-x)}^{2}}\And y=2x\left( 1-x \right) \\ \end{array}$

cancelling $\left( 1-\text{x} \right)$ from both sides

$\begin{array}{*{35}{r}} 1-\text{x} & ~=2\text{x} \\ \Rightarrow \text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ x} & ~=\frac{1}{3} \\ \end{array}$

$\therefore \text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }$ The required function becomes

$f\left( x \right)=\left\{ \begin{array}{*{35}{l}} {{(1-x)}^{2}} & -\infty <x\le \frac{1}{3} \\ 2x\left( 1-x \right) & \frac{1}{3}<x\le \frac{2}{3} \\ {{x}^{2}} & \frac{2}{3}<x<\infty \\ \end{array} \right\}$
